title: BIN Attack Masked as E-commerce Account Creation with Loyalty Enrollment
narrative: Fraudsters exploit retailer signup flows that waive CVV/AVS for first purchase when creating loyalty accounts. Attack targets premium issuer BINs (typically 4-digit BIN 4532, 5471 range) by rapidly creating accounts with sequentially incremented card4 values. Each new account immediately places a $1.00 test transaction followed by a $89-$149 ProductCD=W purchase within 90 seconds. The attacker uses a pool of 15-20 residential proxy IPs (dist1 values 200-850km from merchant addr1) and rotates through disposable email domains matching pattern firstname.lastname@tempmail variants. All transactions occur during standard business hours (txn_hour 9-17) to avoid velocity alerts, with each BIN sequence progressing through 40-60 cards over 4-6 hours before merchant identifies the pattern through chargeback clustering.
indicators: card4 increments by 1-3 digits across 40+ transactions within 6-hour window, same card1 first 6 digits
Dual transaction pattern: $1.00 auth followed by $89-$149 purchase within 60-120 seconds per card
P_emaildomain shows 8+ unique disposable domains (tempmail.com, guerrillamail.info variants) for same merchant session
card1_txn_count_1day shows value of 1 for each card while merchant sees 40+ transactions same BIN
dist1 values cluster in 200-850km range with 15-20 unique addr1 hashes, all residential IP geolocation
All transactions ProductCD=W with identical product SKU, txn_hour consistently 9-17, is_night=0
AVS and CVV response codes show 'U' (unavailable) or 'P' (not processed) for 90%+ of sequence
summary: A fraudster rapidly creates 40-60 loyalty accounts using sequential card numbers from the same BIN prefix, each placing a $1 test charge followed by an $89-$149 purchase within two minutes. All transactions occur during business hours using 15-20 rotating residential proxy IPs, disposable email addresses, and waived CVV/AVS verification on the first purchase.
reasoning: The attacker is testing stolen card ranges (BIN attacks) by exploiting loyalty signup promotions that skip security checks, using the low-dollar auth to confirm active cards before immediately monetizing them. The sequential card numbers, disposable infrastructure, and identical purchase patterns reveal automated credential stuffing rather than legitimate customers organically discovering and joining a rewards program.
indicator_explanations: Card numbers are sequentially generated from the same bank, testing validity
Small auth checks if card works before immediately hitting it harder
Attacker cycles through temporary email services to avoid detection patterns
Each card appears new to the system while merchant sees coordinated barrage
Shipping addresses spread across regions suggest drop networks or stolen credentials
All hits target same product during business hours to blend with normal traffic
Security checks are being bypassed or failing, indicating fraudulent card data
